title: Churros with thick chocolate sauce
subtitle: Fried choux pastry ridges with bitter chocolate dipping sauce
excerpt: Churros: piped choux pastry fried until the ridges are crisp and the interior is soft. Rolled in cinnamon sugar and served with a thick, barely-sweet dark chocolate sauce for dipping. A Spanish breakfast and fair food standard.
sourceNotes: Churros are documented in Spanish and Portuguese culinary tradition from the sixteenth century, with possible origins in Chinese fried dough pastry introduced by Portuguese traders. The choux-like fried dough piped through a star-tipped press appears in Spanish household manuals from the eighteenth century onward. They are consumed throughout Spain for breakfast and at fairs (churrerías) and are documented in public-domain Spanish regional cookbooks. The thick chocolate sauce (chocolate a la taza) is a separate preparation with its own long tradition in Spanish chocolate houses (chocolaterías).

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Churros are best eaten immediately. The chocolate sauce can be made up to 3 days ahead, refrigerated, and reheated gently.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: Deep-fried at 180°C. The ridged star shape of the nozzle creates more surface area; the exterior crisps while the interior stays soft. At lower temperatures the churros are greasy and dough-like at the centre.

glossaryTerms.definition: A choux-adjacent dough: boiling water poured over flour and salt, beaten until a cohesive paste forms. Unlike classic choux, no eggs are added (the dough is less fragile, designed for piping directly into hot oil rather than baking). The consistency should be firm enough to pipe clean ridges and hold its shape when it hits the oil.

Thick Spanish drinking chocolate: cocoa powder or dark chocolate cooked with milk and cornflour to produce a sauce thick enough to coat a spoon. Used for dipping churros. Much thicker than a ganache; it barely flows when hot.

content.text: is faster than choux and needs no eggs. It must be piped immediately while hot and pliable — once it cools it becomes too stiff to pipe cleanly. The star nozzle is essential: smooth-nozzle churros have far less surface area and won't develop the same crunch. The
is thicker than you think is correct; it should cling to the churro rather than run off it.

Whisk the cornflour and cocoa into 100 ml of the cold milk until smooth. Bring the remaining milk, sugar, and a pinch of salt to a simmer in a saucepan. Whisk in the cornflour mixture and cook over medium heat, stirring constantly, until thick — about 3 minutes. Remove from heat and add the chopped chocolate. Stir until smooth and glossy. Keep warm over very low heat.

Combine the flour and salt in a heatproof bowl. Add the boiling water and oil and beat vigorously with a wooden spoon until a cohesive, smooth dough forms — this takes about 2 minutes. The dough will be stiff. Immediately transfer to a piping bag fitted with a large star nozzle. Work quickly; the dough must be piped while hot.

Heat the oil in a large saucepan to 180°C. Pipe churros directly into the oil in 12–15 cm lengths, snipping each off with scissors. Fry 4–5 at a time for 2–3 minutes, turning halfway, until deep amber and the ridges are crisp. Drain briefly on the rack, then roll immediately in the cinnamon sugar mixture. Serve with the warm chocolate sauce for dipping.
